verses.text: At last all the tribes come to Hebron, owning that David is their own flesh and that the LORD had appointed him shepherd of His people, and they anoint him king over all Israel. The promise made years before in a Bethlehem field is fully come. God's word, long delayed, arrives complete.

At last all the tribes come to Hebron, owning that David is their own flesh and that the LORD had appointed him shepherd of His people, and they anoint him king over all Israel. The promise made years before in a Bethlehem field is fully come. God's word, long delayed, arrives complete.

At last all the tribes come to Hebron, owning that David is their own flesh and that the LORD had appointed him shepherd of His people, and they anoint him king over all Israel. The promise made years before in a Bethlehem field is fully come. God's word, long delayed, arrives complete.

David takes the fortress of Zion that the Jebusites boasted no one could enter, and makes it the city of David. The secret is given plainly: 'David went on, and grew great, and the LORD God of hosts was with him.' His rise is not his own doing but God's presence.

David takes the fortress of Zion that the Jebusites boasted no one could enter, and makes it the city of David. The secret is given plainly: 'David went on, and grew great, and the LORD God of hosts was with him.' His rise is not his own doing but God's presence.

David takes the fortress of Zion that the Jebusites boasted no one could enter, and makes it the city of David. The secret is given plainly: 'David went on, and grew great, and the LORD God of hosts was with him.' His rise is not his own doing but God's presence.

David takes the fortress of Zion that the Jebusites boasted no one could enter, and makes it the city of David. The secret is given plainly: 'David went on, and grew great, and the LORD God of hosts was with him.' His rise is not his own doing but God's presence.

Hiram of Tyre sends cedars and builders for David's palace, and David perceives that the LORD had established him as king 'for his people Israel's sake.' He reads his success rightly — as God's gift, given not for his own glory but for the good of God's people.

Hiram of Tyre sends cedars and builders for David's palace, and David perceives that the LORD had established him as king 'for his people Israel's sake.' He reads his success rightly — as God's gift, given not for his own glory but for the good of God's people.

More wives and children are born to David in Jerusalem. Even at the height of blessing, the old weakness persists; the very multiplying God's law warned against goes on. Scripture records the flaw plainly, that we may neither idolize David nor doubt the grace that bore with him.

Hearing David is king, the Philistines come up to seize him, but David 'enquired of the LORD' before he fights, and wins at Baal-perazim. The first move of the new king is to ask God. Power has not made him self-reliant; he still seeks the LORD before every battle.

Hearing David is king, the Philistines come up to seize him, but David 'enquired of the LORD' before he fights, and wins at Baal-perazim. The first move of the new king is to ask God. Power has not made him self-reliant; he still seeks the LORD before every battle.

When the Philistines return, David enquires again, and this time God gives a different plan — wait for the sound in the mulberry trees, for 'the LORD shall go out before thee.' He does not presume yesterday's answer fits today. Fresh obedience seeks fresh guidance for each new day.
